scenario_description: A 50-year-old visitor from the UK at an Airbnb rental. Traveler has a sore throat and difficulty swallowing for two days with no breathing problems.
explanation: Urgent care can test for strep throat and prescribe antibiotics if needed, providing faster service than scheduling a primary care visit.

scenario_description: A 45-year-old visitor from Japan attending a business conference. Visitor fell and hit their head, briefly losing consciousness and now feeling confused.
explanation: Loss of consciousness and confusion after a head injury indicates possible concussion or traumatic brain injury requiring immediate ER evaluation.

scenario_description: A 22-year-old student from South Korea at a national park. Traveler needs cold and flu medicine, throat lozenges, and a thermometer.
explanation: Pharmacies stock a full range of OTC cold remedies, sore throat products, and medical devices like thermometers.

scenario_description: A 30-year-old tourist from Australia during a layover. Visitor needs a prescription transferred from their home country and filled locally.
explanation: Pharmacists can advise on whether foreign prescriptions can be accepted (policies vary by state). A telehealth or urgent care visit may be needed to get a US prescription.

scenario_description: A 50-year-old visitor from the UK visiting family. Visitor needs a prescription transferred from their home country and filled locally.
explanation: Pharmacists can advise on whether foreign prescriptions can be accepted (policies vary by state). A telehealth or urgent care visit may be needed to get a US prescription.

scenario_description: A 40-year-old visitor from Saudi Arabia during a layover. Traveler is experiencing signs of stroke: face drooping, arm weakness, slurred speech.
explanation: FAST symptoms (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911) indicate a stroke. Every minute counts — call 911 immediately.

scenario_description: A 55-year-old visitor from China sightseeing in the city. Traveler needs a prescription renewal for a chronic condition medication they take regularly.
explanation: Telehealth providers can evaluate ongoing chronic conditions and issue prescription renewals in most states. This is the most convenient option for travelers.

scenario_description: A 72-year-old retiree from Canada on a student exchange program. Visitor slipped on wet floor and has wrist pain with minor swelling, no open wound.

scenario_description: A 68-year-old visitor from Italy attending a business conference. Traveler has had a fever of 101°F for 24 hours with mild body aches and no rash.
explanation: Urgent care is appropriate for fevers that are persistent but not dangerously high, allowing evaluation and treatment without ER-level expense.

scenario_description: A 22-year-old student from South Korea attending a business conference. Visitor wants motion sickness medication before a cruise or long road trip.

scenario_description: A 28-year-old traveler from Brazil on a student exchange program. Visitor wants guidance on whether their symptoms warrant an ER visit or can be managed at home.

scenario_description: A 72-year-old retiree from Canada at a hotel. Visitor has a high fever of 104°F with stiff neck and sensitivity to light.
explanation: High fever with neck stiffness and photophobia are classic signs of meningitis, a life-threatening condition. Go to the ER immediately.

scenario_description: A 45-year-old visitor from Japan during a layover. Visitor wants motion sickness medication before a cruise or long road trip.
explanation: OTC options like dimenhydrinate (Dramamine) or meclizine are available at pharmacies for motion sickness prevention.

scenario_description: A 50-year-old visitor from the UK at an Airbnb rental. Visitor has a broken bone with visible deformity after a fall, and extreme pain.
explanation: Suspected fractures with deformity require ER evaluation for imaging, realignment, and possible casting or surgical intervention.

scenario_description: A 50-year-old visitor from the UK at an amusement park. Traveler feels anxious and stressed during their trip and wants to speak with a mental health professional.
explanation: Telehealth platforms offer same-day or next-day mental health counseling via video call. No physical visit required.

scenario_description: A 47-year-old traveler from Portugal sightseeing in the city. Visitor fell and hit their head, briefly losing consciousness and now feeling confused.
explanation: Loss of consciousness and confusion after a head injury indicates possible concussion or traumatic brain injury requiring immediate ER evaluation.

scenario_description: A 72-year-old retiree from Canada at an amusement park. Visitor slipped on wet floor and has wrist pain with minor swelling, no open wound.
explanation: Urgent care can X-ray the wrist to rule out fractures and provide splinting or referral if needed.

scenario_description: A 40-year-old visitor from Saudi Arabia attending a business conference. Traveler is experiencing a seizure for the first time with no prior history of epilepsy.
explanation: A first-time seizure requires immediate ER evaluation to identify the underlying cause.

scenario_description: A 50-year-old visitor from the UK on a student exchange program. Visitor has a deep laceration that is bleeding heavily and not stopping with direct pressure.
explanation: Uncontrolled bleeding from a deep wound is a medical emergency. Go to the ER or call 911 for immediate wound care and possible surgery.

scenario_description: A 62-year-old tourist from Germany at an amusement park. Traveler needs cold and flu medicine, throat lozenges, and a thermometer.
explanation: Pharmacies stock a full range of OTC cold remedies, sore throat products, and medical devices like thermometers.

scenario_description: A 33-year-old tourist from Spain during a layover. Visitor needs antidiarrheal medication after stomach upset from travel.
explanation: OTC medications like loperamide (Imodium) for diarrhea are available at any pharmacy without a prescription.

scenario_description: A 50-year-old visitor from the UK on a 2-week vacation. Traveler is experiencing a seizure for the first time with no prior history of epilepsy.
explanation: A first-time seizure requires immediate ER evaluation to identify the underlying cause.

scenario_description: A 55-year-old visitor from China at a shopping mall. Traveler has had a fever of 101°F for 24 hours with mild body aches and no rash.
explanation: Urgent care is appropriate for fevers that are persistent but not dangerously high, allowing evaluation and treatment without ER-level expense.

scenario_description: A 55-year-old visitor from China on a student exchange program. Visitor has a high fever of 104°F with stiff neck and sensitivity to light.
explanation: High fever with neck stiffness and photophobia are classic signs of meningitis, a life-threatening condition. Go to the ER immediately.

scenario_description: A 35-year-old business traveler from France visiting family. Traveler has had a fever of 101°F for 24 hours with mild body aches and no rash.
explanation: Urgent care is appropriate for fevers that are persistent but not dangerously high, allowing evaluation and treatment without ER-level expense.

scenario_description: A 35-year-old business traveler from France at a shopping mall. Visitor has been vomiting for several hours after eating street food and feels dehydrated.
explanation: Food poisoning with mild-to-moderate dehydration can be treated at urgent care with IV fluids if needed. Go to the ER if symptoms worsen severely.

scenario_description: A 22-year-old student from South Korea attending a business conference. Traveler is experiencing signs of stroke: face drooping, arm weakness, slurred speech.
explanation: FAST symptoms (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911) indicate a stroke. Every minute counts — call 911 immediately.

scenario_description: A 33-year-old tourist from Spain at a hotel. Visitor needs a prescription transferred from their home country and filled locally.
explanation: Pharmacists can advise on whether foreign prescriptions can be accepted (policies vary by state). A telehealth or urgent care visit may be needed to get a US prescription.
